metadata.name: otf2
metadata.summary: OTF2 - The Open Trace Format Version 2

functions.name: python._otf2.ErrorCodes.HandleErrorCode
documentation:
This functions is used for the errcheck attribute of the foreign
functions. To automatically raise an exception if the ErrorCode
returned by the C function is not SUCCESS.

classes.name: python._otf2.ErrorCodes.Error
documentation:
Represents an generic error that occurred when using OTF2 library

This is raised in the case where a C library function returns a value
unlike SUCCESS

FIXME: Just one generic exception class is not much helpful.
Get error description from C library.

modules.name: python._otf2
documentation:
The bindings are very close to the original OTF2 API (See
<https://silc.zih.tu-dresden.de/otf2-current/html/index.html>), with the following exceptions:

0. `OTF2_` is omitted from all names. All symbols are directly in the package.
1. Return codes are checked internally and converted to exceptions of type {py:class}`Error`.
   Error codes are never returned by API functions.
2. Array input parameters that are length / pointer in C, are instead a single sequence
   parameter.
3. Output parameters that are handled by pointers, are instead provided as the return value
   of the function.
   In case of multiple returns outputs, a tuple is returned.
   Some functions also allow to hand in the reference parameters, but that does not work
   for basic python types.

# Enums

Enums are wrapped types, don't treat them as integers. Just assign and compare for (in)equality.
Use == for comparison, not is.

# Callbacks

Callbacks are normal python functions with appropriate arguments similar to those of normal
functions. The caller must ensure that these functions are not garbage collected for the time
that they might be used by OTF2!

:::{warning}
DO NOT pass lambdas or bound methods that go out of scope!
There is no exception here that will save you from attack of the nasal demons.
:::

# Strings

`_otf2` uses the native python str type. Internally OTF2 is defined to use
UTF-8 strings. The conversion happens transparently, a user shall hand `str`
objects to the API and will always receive `str` objects by the API.

# ctypes

`_otf2` uses ctypes. Some of the types of the module are aliases to ctypes-types, but you never
need to explicitly use ctypes.

modules.name: python._otf2.AttributeValue
documentation: Generated by OTF2 Template Engine

classes.name: python._otf2.AttributeValue.AttributeValue
documentation:
ctypes does not support unions as (non-pointer) arguments
_arg_value / _arg_type is a crude and evil hack to guess
the right argument type. This type must be correct in terms of
size (maximum of the union) and
calling convention (architecture specific, but for AMD64 int usually wins).
Whenever used as a direct function argument (also applies to callbacks)
this type should be used instead of the union. Otherwise you get undefined
behavior.

functions.name: python._otf2.Config.str_errcheck_nullable
documentation:
ctypes errcheck method for strings.
NULL / None is ok.
Use in combination with restype = ctypes.c_char_p
Python3 variant, decodes the binary string to unicode

functions.name: python._otf2.Config.str_decode
documentation:
Decode a string from otf2 / ctypes.
Asserts the input is a valid string or NULL.
Python3 variant, decodes the binary string to unicode

classes.name: python._otf2.Config.StrParam
documentation:
argtype for ctypes to be used for string pointers
Python3 variant, encodes the unicode to binary

It converts a a _field between list of Definitions and list of references

methods.name: python.otf2.definitions.Group.rank
documentation:
Get the rank / index of a given {py:class}`Location` or any other contained Definition with
respect to this group.

methods.name: python.otf2.definitions.Group.location
documentation: Get the {py:class}`Location` or other Definition object for a given rank within this group

Groups of type COMM_GROUP may be constructed from a sequence of either ranks or location
objects as members argument.
All other group types must be constructed using a sequence of their respective Definition
objects.
The members property is always a sequence of Definition objects, even when the Group is
constructed using a sequence of ranks.

classes.name: python.otf2.definitions.Parameter
documentation: def rank(self, location):
Get the rank / index of a given :py:class:`Location` with respect to this communicator.
return self.group.rank(location)
def location(self, rank):
Get the :py:class:`Location` for a given rank within this communicator
return self.group.location(rank)

methods.name: python.otf2.registry._ListRegistry.__iter__
documentation: Do not try to add elements during iteration

classes.name: python.otf2.registry._RefRegistry

methods.name: python.otf2.registry._RefRegistry.__iter__
documentation: Do not try to add elements during iteration

All objects recorded by this registry must have:
- a ref (id) of type int as first constructor parameter.
  This must not be changed once an event with that ref is written in the trace
- a name of type str as a second constructor parameter.
  If this is changed, the _elements_by_name must be updated

classes.name: python.otf2.registry._StringRegistry

methods.name: python.otf2.registry._StringRegistry.get_ref
documentation:
Get a definition object, and write it in the writer if it was not yet created.
This means, that all previously created definitions should already be written to the writer.
Returns the ref of the definition for simplicity.
This is a special registry for strings.

functions.name: python.otf2.reader.open
documentation:
Returns a {py:class}`otf2.reader.Reader` object usable within a {py:obj}`with` statement.

```
param str anchor_file_path

the path to the anchor file (mostly 'some/path/trace.otf2')

param int batch_events

the number of events, which are batched while reading the events
from the trace (default: 100)
```
The contextlib.closing documentation actually suggests to name this thing open
so we do this despite the shadowing

classes.name: python.otf2.reader.Reader

methods.name: python.otf2.reader.Reader.timer_resolution
documentation: Returns the resolution of the timer of the read trace

methods.name: python.otf2.reader.Reader.events
documentation:
Returns an iterable object representing all events in the opened trace.

Example:

```python
for location, event in trace.events(location_foo):
    print("Encountered event at {}".format(event.time))
```

```
param Iterable locations

all locations, for which events will be read, (None means for every location)
```

methods.name: python.otf2.reader.Reader.definitions
documentation:
Returns a {py:class}`otf2.registry.DefinitionRegistry` object representing all
definitions in the trace.

methods.name: python.otf2.reader.Reader.handle
documentation: Returns the handle to the underlaying OTF2 Reader.

This class is used to read existing traces

:param str anchor_file_path: the path to the anchor file (mostly 'some/path/trace.otf2')
:param int batch_events: the number of events, which are batched while reading the events
        from the trace (default: 100)

modules.name: python.otf2.events
documentation: Generated by OTF2 Template Engine

classes.name: python.otf2.events.BufferFlush
documentation:
This event signals that the internal buffer was flushed at the given
time.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.MeasurementOnOff
documentation:
This event signals where the measurement system turned measurement on or
off.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.Enter
documentation: An @e Enter record indicates that the program enters a code region.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.Leave
documentation: A @e Leave record indicates that the program leaves a code region.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.MpiSend
documentation:
An @e MpiSend record indicates that an MPI send operation was initiated
(MPI_SEND). It keeps the necessary information for this event: receiver
of the message, communicator, and the message tag. You can optionally add
further information like the message length (size of the send buffer).

classes.name: python.otf2.events.MpiIsend
documentation:
An @e MpiIsend record indicates that a non-blocking MPI send operation was
initiated (MPI_ISEND). It keeps the necessary information for this event:
receiver of the message, communicator, and the message tag. You can
optionally add further information like the message length (size of the
send buffer).

classes.name: python.otf2.events.MpiIsendComplete
documentation:
An @e MpiIsendComplete record indicates the completion of a non-blocking MPI
send operation. In the case where the send request is released before it is
completed by MPI, this record will only indicate the release, as it becomes
impossible to track the completion of the send operation afterwards. This
case may be identified by the surrounding events.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.MpiIrecvRequest
documentation:
An @e MpiIrecvRequest record indicates that a non-blocking MPI receive
operation was initiated (MPI_IRECV).

classes.name: python.otf2.events.MpiRecv
documentation:
An @e MpiRecv record indicates that an MPI message was received (MPI_RECV). It
keeps the necessary information for this event: sender of the message,
communicator, and the message tag. You can optionally add further
information like the message length (size of the receive buffer).

classes.name: python.otf2.events.MpiIrecv
documentation:
An @e MpiIrecv record indicates the completion of a non-blocking MPI receive
operation completed (MPI_IRECV). It keeps the necessary information for this
event: sender of the message, communicator, and the message tag. You can
optionally add further information like the message length (size of the
receive buffer).

classes.name: python.otf2.events.MpiRequestTest
documentation:
This events appears if the program tests if a request has already completed
but the test failed.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.MpiRequestCancelled
documentation: This events appears if the program canceled a request.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.MpiCollectiveBegin
documentation:
An @e MpiCollectiveBegin record marks the begin of an MPI collective operation
(MPI_GATHER, MPI_SCATTER etc.).

classes.name: python.otf2.events.MpiCollectiveEnd
documentation:
An @e MpiCollectiveEnd record marks the end of an MPI collective operation
(MPI_GATHER, MPI_SCATTER etc.). It keeps the necessary information for this
event: type of collective operation, communicator, the root of this
collective operation. You can optionally add further information like sent
and received bytes.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.OmpFork
documentation:
An @e OmpFork record marks that an OpenMP Thread forks a thread team.

This event record is superseded by the @eref{ThreadFork} event record and
should not be used when the @eref{ThreadFork} event record is in use.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.OmpJoin
documentation:
An @e OmpJoin record marks that a team of threads is joint and only the master
thread continues execution.

This event record is superseded by the @eref{ThreadJoin} event record and
should not be used when the @eref{ThreadJoin} event record is in use.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.OmpAcquireLock
documentation:
An @e OmpAcquireLock record marks that a thread acquires an OpenMP lock.

This event record is superseded by the @eref{ThreadAcquireLock} event
record and should not be used when the @eref{ThreadAcquireLock} event
record is in use.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.OmpReleaseLock
documentation:
An @e OmpReleaseLock record marks that a thread releases an OpenMP lock.

This event record is superseded by the @eref{ThreadReleaseLock} event
record and should not be used when the @eref{ThreadReleaseLock} event
record is in use.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.OmpTaskCreate
documentation:
An @e OmpTaskCreate record marks that an OpenMP Task was/will be created in
the current region.

This event record is superseded by the @eref{ThreadTaskCreate} event record
and should not be used when the @eref{ThreadTaskCreate} event record is in
use.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.OmpTaskSwitch
documentation:
An @e OmpTaskSwitch record indicates that the execution of the current task
will be suspended and another task starts/restarts its execution.
Please note that this may change the current call stack of the executing
location.

This event record is superseded by the @eref{ThreadTaskSwitch} event record
and should not be used when the @eref{ThreadTaskSwitch} event record is in
use.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.OmpTaskComplete
documentation:
An @e OmpTaskComplete record indicates that the execution of an OpenMP task
has finished.

This event record is superseded by the @eref{ThreadTaskComplete} event
record and should not be used when the @eref{ThreadTaskComplete} event
record is in use.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.Metric
documentation: This event represents a measurement point.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ParameterString
documentation:
A ParameterString record marks that in the current region, the specified
string parameter has the specified value.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ParameterInt
documentation:
A @e ParameterInt record marks that in the current region, the specified
integer parameter has the specified value.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ParameterUnsignedInt
documentation:
A @e ParameterUnsignedInt record marks that in the current region, the
specified unsigned integer parameter has the specified value.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaWinCreate
python.otf2.events.RmaWinDestroy
documentation:
An @e RmaWinDestroy record denotes the destruction of an RMA window. Only
valid if the @eref{RmaWin} definition was flagged with
@eref{OTF2_RMA_WIN_FLAG_CREATE_DESTROY_EVENTS}. This event can be enclosed
by an @eref{RmaCollectiveBegin} and @eref{RmaCollectiveEnd} event pair
either with @eref{OTF2_COLLECTIVE_OP_DESTROY_HANDLE} or
@eref{OTF2_COLLECTIVE_OP_DESTROY_HANDLE_AND_DEALLOCATE} as the operation type.
In this case the RMA window is only marked for destruction, which will happen
with the @eref{RmaCollectiveEnd} event.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaCollectiveBegin
documentation:
An @e RmaCollectiveBegin record denotes the beginning of a collective RMA
operation.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaCollectiveEnd
documentation: An @e RmaCollectiveEnd record denotes the end of a collective RMA operation.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaGroupSync
documentation:
An @e RmaGroupSync record denotes the synchronization with a subgroup of
processes on an RMA window.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaRequestLock
documentation:
An @e RmaRequestLock record denotes the time a lock was requested and with
it the earliest time it could have been granted.  It is used to mark
(possibly) non-blocking lock request, as defined by the MPI standard.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaAcquireLock
documentation:
An @e RmaAcquireLock record denotes the time a lock was acquired by the
process.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaTryLock
documentation:
An @e RmaTryLock record denotes the time of an unsuccessful attempt to
acquire the lock.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaReleaseLock
documentation: An @e RmaReleaseLock record denotes the time the lock was released.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaSync
documentation:
An @e RmaSync record denotes the direct synchronization with a possibly
remote process.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaWaitChange
documentation:
An @e RmaWaitChange record denotes the change of a window that was waited
for.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaPut
documentation: An @e RmaPut record denotes the time a put operation was issued.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaGet
documentation: An @e RmaGet record denotes the time a get operation was issued.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaAtomic
documentation: An @e RmaAtomic record denotes the time an atomic RMA operation was issued.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaOpCompleteBlocking
documentation:
An @e RmaOpCompleteBlocking record denotes the local completion of a
blocking RMA operation.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaOpCompleteNonBlocking
documentation:
An @e RmaOpCompleteNonBlocking record denotes the local completion of a
non-blocking RMA operation.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaOpTest
documentation:
An @e RmaOpTest record denotes that a non-blocking RMA operation has been
tested for completion unsuccessfully.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.RmaOpCompleteRemote
documentation:
An @e RmaOpCompleteRemote record denotes the remote completion of an RMA
operation.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadFork
documentation: A @e ThreadFork record marks that a thread forks a thread team.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadJoin
documentation:
A @e ThreadJoin record marks that a team of threads is joint and only the
master thread continues execution.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadTeamBegin
documentation: The current location enters the specified thread team.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadTeamEnd
documentation: The current location leaves the specified thread team.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadAcquireLock
documentation: A @e ThreadAcquireLock record marks that a thread acquires a lock.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadReleaseLock
documentation: A @e ThreadReleaseLock record marks that a thread releases a lock.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadTaskCreate
documentation:
A @e ThreadTaskCreate record marks that a task in was/will be created and
will be processed by the specified thread team.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadTaskSwitch
documentation:
A @e ThreadTaskSwitch record indicates that the execution of the current
task will be suspended and another task starts/restarts its execution.
Please note that this may change the current call stack of the executing
location.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadTaskComplete
documentation:
A @e ThreadTaskComplete record indicates that the execution of an OpenMP
task has finished.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadCreate
documentation: The location created successfully a new thread.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadBegin
documentation: Marks the begin of a thread created by another thread.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadWait
documentation: The location waits for the completion of another thread.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ThreadEnd
documentation: Marks the end of a thread.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.CallingContextEnter
documentation:
The thread entered an instrumented region, represented by the referenced
@eref{CallingContext}. In contrast to the @eref{Enter} event, it gives
the full calling context through the @eref{CallingContext} tree.

Events based on the @eref{CallingContext} definition are mutually exclusive
with the @eref{Enter}/@eref{Leave} events in a trace.

If no callback for this event is set but a callback for @eref{Enter} events
is defined, the reader will automatically generate an @eref{Enter} callback
call for the @eref{Region} referenced by the @eref{CallingContext} attribute
of this event.  Note that this emulation does @b not re-create the full
calling context!  It only re-creates the event order for instrumented
regions.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.CallingContextLeave
documentation:
The thread left an instrumented region, represented by the referenced
@eref{CallingContext}. In contrast to the @eref{Leave} event, it gives
the full calling context through the @eref{CallingContext} tree.

The unwind distance for this @eref{CallingContext} is defined to be 1.
Because it must be assumed that the instrumented region made progress
since the previous @eref{CallingContext} event.

Events based on the @eref{CallingContext} definition are mutually exclusive
with the @eref{Enter}/@eref{Leave} events in a trace.

The parent of the @eref{CallingContext} must be used as the previous calling
context for the next event.

If no callback for this event is set but a callback for @eref{Leave} events
is defined, the reader will automatically generate an @eref{Leave} callback
call for the @eref{Region} referenced by the @eref{CallingContext} attribute
of this event.  Note that this emulation does @b not re-create the full
calling context!  It only re-creates the event order for instrumented
regions.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.CallingContextSample
documentation:
The thread was interrupted to take a sample of its current state (region and
source code location).

Events based on the @eref{CallingContext} definition are mutually exclusive
with the @eref{Enter}/@eref{Leave} events in a trace.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.IoCreateHandle
python.otf2.events.IoDestroyHandle
python.otf2.events.IoDuplicateHandle
python.otf2.events.IoSeek
documentation: An @e IoSeek record marks a change of the position, e.g., within a file.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.IoChangeStatusFlags
documentation:
An @e IoChangeStatusFlags record marks a change to the status flags
associated with an @emph{active} I/O handle.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.IoDeleteFile
documentation: An @e IoDeleteFile record marks the deletion of an I/O file.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.IoOperationBegin
documentation:
An @e IoOperationBegin record marks the begin of a file operation (read,
write, etc.).

See @ref otf2_io_operation_event_order for the possible event orders.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.IoOperationTest
documentation:
An @e IoOperationTest record marks an unsuccessful test whether an I/O
operation has already finished.

See @ref otf2_io_operation_event_order for the possible event orders.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.IoOperationIssued
documentation:
An @e IoOperationIssued record marks the successful initiation of a
non-blocking operation (read, write, etc.) on an @emph{active} I/O handle.

See @ref otf2_io_operation_event_order for the possible event orders.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.IoOperationComplete
documentation:
An @e IoOperationComplete record marks the end of a file operation
(read, write, etc.) on an @emph{active} I/O handle.

See @ref otf2_io_operation_event_order for the possible event orders.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.IoOperationCancelled
documentation:
An @e IoOperationCancelled record marks the successful cancellation of a
non-blocking operation (read, write, etc.) on an @emph{active} I/O handle.

See @ref otf2_io_operation_event_order for the possible event orders.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.IoAcquireLock
documentation: An @e IoAcquireLock record marks the acquisition of an I/O lock.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.IoReleaseLock
documentation: An @e IoReleaseLock record marks the release of an I/O lock.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.IoTryLock
documentation: An @e IoTryLock record marks when an I/O lock was requested but not granted.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.ProgramBegin
python.otf2.events.ProgramEnd
python.otf2.events.NonBlockingCollectiveRequest
documentation:
A @e NonBlockingCollectiveRequest record indicates that a non-blocking
collective operation was initiated.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.NonBlockingCollectiveComplete
documentation:
A @e NonBlockingCollectiveComplete record indicates that a non-blocking
collective operation completed.

classes.name: python.otf2.events.CommCreate
python.otf2.events.CommDestroy
documentation:
A @e CommDestroy record marks the communicator for destruction at the end of
the enclosing @eref{MpiCollectiveBegin} and @eref{MpiCollectiveEnd} event
pair. Only valid if the @eref{Comm} definition was flagged with
@eref{OTF2_COMM_FLAG_CREATE_DESTROY_EVENTS}. This event must be enclosed
by an @eref{MpiCollectiveBegin} and @eref{MpiCollectiveEnd} event pair
with @eref{OTF2_COLLECTIVE_OP_DESTROY_HANDLE} as the operation type.

functions.name: python.otf2.writer.open
documentation:
Returns a {py:class}`otf2.reader.Reader` object usable within a {py:obj}`with`
statement.

```
param str anchor_path

the path to the trace root directory

param str archive_name

the name of the trace archive (default: "traces")
```
The contextlib.closing documentation actually suggests to name this thing open
so we do this despite the shadowing

methods.name: python.otf2.writer.Writer.definitions
documentation:
Provides access to the global trace definitions.
In the parallel case, all calls to the definitions must be collective.

```
return

```

methods.name: python.otf2.writer.Writer.timer_resolution
documentation: Returns the resolution of the used timer

methods.name: python.otf2.writer.Writer.handle
documentation: Returns the handle to the underlaying OTF2 Archive.

documentation: setter/getter

methods.name: python.otf2.writer.Writer.is_primary
documentation: Read only

methods.name: python.otf2.writer.Writer.is_master
documentation: Read only, deprecated

This class is used to write a new trace file.

:::{Warning}
Parallel trace writing using collective callbacks is currently not supported.
:::

```
param str anchor_path

the path to the trace root directory

param str archive_name

the name of the trace archive (default: 'traces')

param int timer_resolution

the resolution of the used timer (default: 1)

param otf2.registry.DefinitionRegistry definitions

used to share definitions with
other  and  (default: None)
```
